disease.name: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD)
disease.note: COPD is characterised by persistent respiratory symptoms and airflow limitation due to airway and/or alveolar abnormalities. GOLD criteria define diagnosis by post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.70. CAT and mMRC scales assess symptom burden.

additionalCriteria.description: ≥2 exacerbations in prior year or ≥1 hospitalisation

historyFeatures.note: Often first symptom; may be intermittent and unproductive
Variable intensity; may be absent in emphysema-predominant
Acute worsening of respiratory symptoms requiring treatment change

riskFactors.note: Most important risk factor; pack-years correlates with severity
Indoor cooking smoke in developing nations

subtypes.name: Emphysema-predominant COPD (Pink Puffer)
subtypes.management: Bronchodilators; lung volume reduction surgery if upper lobe predominant; consider bullectomy

subtypes.name: Chronic Bronchitis-predominant COPD (Blue Bloater)
subtypes.management: Bronchodilators; inhaled corticosteroids if frequent exacerbations; LTOT if hypoxaemic; pulmonary rehabilitation

subtypes.name: COPD GOLD Group E (High Exacerbation Risk)
subtypes.management: LAMA + LABA + ICS triple therapy; roflumilast in chronic bronchitis with frequent exacerbations; azithromycin

bedside.name: Pulse oximetry
bedside.indication: all patients at initial diagnosis and exacerbation

laboratory.name: Arterial blood gas
laboratory.priority: if FEV1 <50% or exacerbation with hypoxia
laboratory.note: Acute exacerbation: check pH, PaCO2, PaO2, HCO3 to guide NIV or ventilation

laboratory.name: Alpha-1 antitrypsin level
laboratory.priority: if onset <45 years or minimal smoking history

laboratory.name: FBC
laboratory.indication: polycythaemia suggests chronic hypoxia; eosinophil count guides ICS use

imaging.name: Chest X-ray PA and lateral
imaging.indication: exclude alternative diagnoses; assess hyperinflation, bullae

imaging.name: CT chest (high resolution)
imaging.priority: if surgery considered or diagnostic uncertainty
imaging.indication: quantify emphysema; assess for bronchiectasis; lung volume reduction workup

advanced.name: Pulmonary function tests (spirometry, lung volumes, DLCO)
advanced.note: Post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.70 confirms COPD. GOLD 1: FEV1 ≥80%, GOLD 2: 50–79%, GOLD 3: 30–49%, GOLD 4: <30%

advanced.name: Six-minute walk test
advanced.indication: assess exercise capacity and need for pulmonary rehab or LTOT

advanced.name: Echocardiogram
advanced.indication: if cor pulmonale or pulmonary hypertension suspected

management.protocolReference: GOLD 2024 Report; NICE Guideline NG115; ATS/ERS COPD Guidelines

pathways.criteria: FEV1 ≥50%, mMRC 0–1, CAT <10, 0–1 exacerbations/year, low symptoms
treatment: Smoking cessation counselling and pharmacotherapy (varenicline, NRT, bupropion)
Short-acting bronchodilator PRN (SABA: salbutamol, or SAMA: ipratropium)

pathways.criteria: FEV1 30–49%, mMRC ≥2, CAT ≥10, 0–1 exacerbations/year, more symptoms
treatment: LAMA + LABA is initial dual therapy (more effective than monotherapy per GOLD)
Pulmonary rehabilitation (improves exercise capacity, QOL, reduces hospitalisation)
Self-management plan including action plan for exacerbations
pathways.followUp: Every 6 months; spirometry, CAT, exacerbation history

pathways.criteria: FEV1 <50%, ≥2 exacerbations or ≥1 hospitalisation, high symptom burden
treatment: Triple therapy: LAMA + LABA + ICS (fluticasone/umeclidinium/vilanterol, budesonide/glycopyrronium/formoterol)
Consider blood eosinophils ≥300 cells/μL for ICS benefit; avoid ICS if frequent pneumonia
Long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT) if PaO2 <7.3 kPa (55 mmHg) or <8 kPa with cor pulmonale, use ≥15 hours/day
Consider roflumilast (PDE-4 inhibitor) if chronic bronchitis with frequent exacerbations despite triple therapy
Consider chronic macrolide (azithromycin) if exacerbations persist
Assess for lung volume reduction (endobronchial valves, bullectomy, LVRS) in emphysema-predominant
Lung transplant evaluation if progressive disease, BODE index 7–10
pathways.escalation: Exacerbation management: bronchodilators, steroids, antibiotics if purulent sputum. NIV for hypercapnic respiratory failure. ICU if NIV fails or acidosis worsens.

complications.management: Bronchodilators, systemic corticosteroids (prednisolone 30–40 mg for 5 days), antibiotics if purulent sputum
NIV first-line for hypercapnic failure; intubate if NIV fails or contraindicated

disease.emergencyPriority: if acute exacerbation with respiratory acidosis or altered consciousness, urgent NIV or ICU
diagnosticClues: progressive exertional dyspnoea with smoking history
hyperinflation on CXR with flattened diaphragms
frequent exacerbations requiring antibiotics or steroids
exclusionClues: significant bronchodilator reversibility >400 mL suggests asthma
fine basal crackles and restrictive pattern suggest ILD
large volume purulent sputum and HRCT changes suggest bronchiectasis
isolated DLCO reduction with normal spirometry suggests pulmonary vascular disease
